4 GROWTH ZIPS · 5 CORE ZIPS

El Paso Housing Ledger

Geography Housing Form Amenities Value Zip Profiles Old Core vs. New Growth Methodology
El Paso, Texas · 2010–2014 vs 2020–2025

Four growth zips, five historic ones — and two very different housing stories.

A parcel-level look at 17,113 single-family homes built across El Paso's four highest-growth suburban tracts in two five-year windows, compared over time — plus a new comparison against 817 parcels in the city's historic central core, to see what actually separates new construction from the old city.

GROWTH-AREA DATA: RENTCAST.IO·CENTRAL-CORE DATA: REGRID.COM
2010–2014 2020–2025
Each point is one parcel, plotted by its actual latitude / longitude.
Section 01 · Growth Areas

New construction pulled back everywhere, but 79938 is still the engine of El Paso's growth.

Across all four zips, new single-family construction fell 31.8% between the two windows — but nowhere close to the near-total collapse a partial version of this dataset once suggested. The far east is down, not gone.

New homes built, by zip code

Count of homes with a recorded year-built in each window.

Share of new construction, by zip code

79938 remains the dominant zip in both periods.

Homes built per year, by zip code

Covers the two five-year windows this analysis is scoped to: 2010–2014 and 2020–2025.
  • 79938 (Far East: Tierra del Este) is still, by far, the largest source of new homes — 6,998 in 2010–2014, 4,562 in 2020–2025 (‑34.8%). It supplied 69% of all new construction in the earlier window and 66% in the later one.
  • 79911 (Northwest: Upper Valley/Cimarron) is essentially flat — 1,050 to 1,041 homes — and now builds every year of the 2020s window rather than being concentrated in one year.
  • 79934 (Northeast: Campo Del Sol/Sandstone Ranch/Mesquite Hills) — new to this analysis — declined 29.3% (1,685 → 1,192), holding a steady 16–17% share of new construction in both periods.
  • 79936 (East-Central: Pebble Hills/Vista Real) saw the sharpest percentage decline of the four, down 66.7% (439 → 146), consistent with a built-out area running out of vacant infill parcels.
  • A data correction worth flagging: an earlier pass of this analysis, built on an incomplete extract, showed 79938 with zero recorded homes from 2020–2024 and a implausible single-year spike in 2025. The completed dataset shows steady year-by-year activity throughout the 2020s instead — a reminder that data gaps can look like real trends until the record is filled in.

Size and value, year by year

Average home size and assessed value, plotted year over year rather than as two-bucket averages.

Average square footage by year

Covers the two five-year windows this analysis is scoped to.

Average assessed value by year

Breaks in each line mark years/zips with no assessment data on file.
Section 02 · Growth Areas

What's being built: bigger, denser homes with fewer bedrooms.

Average size, bedroom count, and price all rose across the board. Lot sizes barely moved — meaning new homes are occupying more of their lots than a decade ago.

Filter distributions by zip

Bedroom count

Distribution of homes by bedroom count.

Square footage

Distribution of finished square footage.

Lot size

Distribution of lot size (capped at 16,000 sqft for readability).

Floor-area ratio (density proxy)

Average finished sqft ÷ lot sqft, by zip. Higher = house occupies more of the lot.
Average lot size barely changed (6,974 → 6,965 sqft citywide across the four zips) while average square footage rose 12% — so the density ratio climbed from 0.301 to 0.323. New homes aren't spreading out; they're filling in more of a similarly-sized lot.
Section 03 · Growth Areas

Newer homes come with more garage, far fewer fireplaces.

This update brings in parcel-level amenity data for the first time. Garages and central cooling are close to universal in both periods; fireplaces have all but disappeared from newer listings. Coverage varies a lot by feature — see the notes on each chart.

Homes reporting a fireplace

Share of homes with feature data that report having a fireplace. Coverage of this specific field is thin (≈19% of 2010–2014 homes, ≈4% of 2020–2025 homes have it reported at all) — treat the direction, not the exact size, as the finding.

Average garage spaces & total rooms

Among homes reporting each field. Garage capacity ticked up slightly; total room count fell even as bedroom count rose — newer homes pack more bedrooms into fewer overall rooms.
Garage (reported in ~85% of homes) and central cooling (reported in ~83%) are effectively standard in both periods — expected in a hot, car-dependent desert market. They're not useful for telling old construction from new; fireplace prevalence and room efficiency are.
Section 04 · Growth Areas

Assessed values rose faster than square footage — but coverage is uneven.

Where county assessment data exists, new homes are worth considerably more per square foot than a decade ago. Some of the newest parcels haven't been assessed yet, so treat these as directional.

Median assessed value, by zip

Most recent available county assessment per parcel.

Assessed value per square foot

Average estimated value ÷ square footage.
Section 05 · Growth Areas

Owner-occupancy stayed high and rose slightly, citywide.

Across all four growth zips, owner-occupancy rose from 91.1% to 93.8% — a modest but consistent increase, not a decline.

Owner-occupancy rate

Section 06 · Growth Areas

Four zip codes, four growth stories.

Section 07 · Macro Comparison

Old core vs. new growth: what actually differs?

A second dataset — parcel records from Regrid.com covering El Paso's historic central zips (79901, 79902, 79903, 79905, 79930) — lets us compare the two geographies directly. The central-core file tracks homes built or renovated continuously from 2010–2024, so it isn't split into two windows the way the growth-area data is; the comparison below treats each region as a single pool.

Central-core property type

Growth-suburb figures are single-family only in every view (100% of that dataset). Single-family is the default for the central core, to keep the comparison apples-to-apples with the growth suburbs; switch to see how including multifamily changes the picture.

New Growth Suburbs

4 zips · RentCast.io

vs.
Historic Central Core

5 zips · Regrid.com

Average square footage

Nearly identical on average — the real difference is elsewhere.

Average lot size

Central-core lots run larger on average, pulled up by a few big historic parcels.

Floor-area ratio (density)

Growth-area homes occupy more of their lot despite similar house size.

Assessed value: mean vs. median

Central-core values are pulled up by a handful of high-value parcels — its median sits well below its mean.

Property type mix

Growth areas are 100% single-family. The central core carries a real multifamily share.
  • Average home size is nearly a wash — 1,895 sqft in the growth suburbs vs. 1,870 sqft in the central core, only a 1.3% gap. The "new homes are bigger" story mostly doesn't hold up against the old core as a whole.
  • But medians tell a different story: central-core median square footage (1,607 sqft) is noticeably below its mean (1,870 sqft), pointing to a skewed mix of modest bungalows alongside a smaller number of large historic homes — particularly in 79901 and 79902. The growth suburbs are more uniform.
  • Central-core lots are actually larger on average — 8,320 sqft vs. 6,970 sqft in the growth suburbs (+19% relatively) — driven by spacious older lots in 79902. Combined with similar house sizes, that gives the central core a meaningfully lower density ratio (0.263 vs. 0.310).
  • Growth-suburb homes carry a higher assessed value — mean $288,523 vs. $252,064 in the central core (+14.5%) — and a higher price per square foot ($154.21 vs. $134.51, +14.6%).
  • The central core is meaningfully more multifamily — 18% of central-core parcels are duplex-to-fourplex structures, versus 0% in the growth suburbs, which are exclusively single-family by definition of the source data.
  • Multifamily central-core parcels run more expensive, not cheaper — averaging $398,166 assessed value and $185.60/sqft, both well above the central core's single-family average. Switch the toggle above to "Multifamily Only" or "Both" to see this shift the central-core figures upward.
  • 79902 (Kern Place / Sunset Heights-adjacent) is the outlier within the core — its homes average 2,694 sqft and $458,987 in assessed value, well above every other central zip and even above most growth-suburb zips. The rest of the central core (79901, 79903, 79905, 79930) is considerably more modest, averaging under 1,900 sqft and under $230,000.

These figures describe the single-family comparison (the toggle's default). Use the toggle above to see how the central-core numbers shift with multifamily included.

Inside the central core: five zips, one historic downtown

Sample sizes are small — read as directional.

Single-family homes built or renovated per year

Methodology & data notes

How this analysis was built.

Growth-area source: Parcel-level property records for El Paso, Texas, provided by RentCast.io. This update replaces the original extract with a corrected, completed file: it fills in previously-missing assessment, zoning, and ownership data for zip 79938 and adds a fourth growth zip, 79934 (Northeast: Campo Del Sol / Sandstone Ranch / Mesquite Hills). A fifth zip present in the raw file, 79932, was not part of the four growth geographies this analysis is scoped to. This analysis is deliberately scoped to single-family homes built in one of two five-year windows — 2010–2014 and 2020–2025 — to compare the earlier growth boom against the current decade. All 17,113 homes in this file are single-family.

Central-core source: Parcel records for five historic central El Paso zips (79901, 79902, 79903, 79905, 79930), provided by Regrid.com. This dataset spans a continuous 2010–2024 build/renovation year, and includes both single-family homes (82% of records, usecode A1) and small multifamily structures (18%, usecodes B1–B4, i.e. duplex through fourplex). Use the single-family / multifamily / both toggle in the macro comparison section to control which of these are included in central-core figures; single-family is the default, to keep the comparison to the growth areas apples-to-apples.

Estimated / assessed value: For both sources, "value" means the most recent available county assessment, not a sale price. Sale price is recorded for under 2% of growth-area parcels and for only 7 of 670 central-core single-family parcels (the rest are recorded as $0, a placeholder for "no qualifying sale on file"), so sale price was excluded from both analyses as unreliable.

Amenities: Growth-area parcels include a features field (garage, fireplace, cooling, room count, etc.) not present in earlier versions of this analysis. This field is sparse and inconsistently reported — for example, fireplace status is only recorded for ~19% of 2010–2014 homes and ~4% of 2020–2025 homes — so amenity rates in this report are calculated among homes that report each field, with reporting counts shown alongside. The central-core source has no equivalent parcel-level amenity data, so amenities are not compared across regions.

Bedroom coverage in the central core is also incomplete (399 of 670 single-family parcels, ~60%), and living-area square footage comes from the parcel's recorded building area rather than a standardized "finished square footage" field, so size comparisons between the two sources should be read as approximate.

Zoning: Zoning codes are missing for 78% of 2020–2025 growth-area parcels, so zoning is not used as a basis for conclusions in the growth-area sections. Zoning descriptions are more complete in the central-core file and are summarized narratively where relevant.

Floor-area ratio (FAR): Calculated as finished/building square footage ÷ lot size for each parcel, then averaged. Used here as a simple proxy for how much of each lot is occupied by the structure — not a formal zoning FAR calculation.

Data: RentCast.io (growth areas: 79911, 79934, 79936, 79938) & Regrid.com (central core: 79901, 79902, 79903, 79905, 79930) · El Paso, TX
Built for internal policy analysis. Figures are derived from available county records and may undercount recent, unassessed construction.
